explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to **news reporting, official statements, and journalistic narratives** (such as legal proceedings with named entities, government announcements, sports reports with citations, cultural event descriptions, or investigative journalism pieces).

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears across multiple languages (English, Spanish, Dutch, Swedish, Turkish) and spans diverse topics: environmental incidents, legal judgments, sports updates, entertainment news, political trade statements, and local event coverage. It consistently includes specific names, dates, sources (e.g., “According to a Morning Sports report”), quotations, and detailed contextual explanations.

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: High-activation texts feature elaborated reporting with citations, specific entities, and narrative detail, while low-activation examples are often generic headlines, incomplete leads, or simple statements lacking such journalistic framing, named sources, or contextual depth.

4. **Closing interpretation**: The neuron detects text that exhibits the stylistic and structural hallmarks of factual reporting, focusing on formal, sourced, and context-rich descriptions typical of news articles or official communications.

results.plain_text: Three mines fail to comply with obligations to help Sekhukhune community — report  The department of minerals, resources and energy failed to adequately regulate mines and carry out its oversight role to ensure the implementation of community development plans in the Sekhukhune region in Limpopo. This is the finding contained in a research report released by civil society organisations Amnesty International SA, Centre for Applied Legal Studies and the Sekhukhune Combined Mining-Affected Communities yesterday. The department, which is the regulating body of mining rights and enforcement of social and labour plans (SLPs), failed to ensure their implementation in Sekhukhune by mining companies. A SLP is a document which mining companies are required to submit to the minerals and energy department outlining how a mine will benefit communities affected by the mining operation. The report was launched in a virtual event attended by members of the civil organisations under the theme: “Unearthing the truth: have the mines failed the communities in the Sekhukhune region in SA?”  The research focused on the state of compliance of SLPs by three mining companies operating in the Sekhukhune area and experiences of local mining-affected communities. The research was conducted between October 2020 and July 2021. The three mining companies investigated were Twickenham platinum mine — a wholly owned subsidiary of Anglo American Platinum Ltd which is the world’s largest primary producer of platinum, Marula platinum mine — owned by Impala Platinum Holdings Ltd, and Sefateng chrome mine. The report found that all the three mining companies investigated had failed to comply with obligations which arose from respective SLPs. The report found that Twickenham failed to complete a project related to water and sanitation at schools. Marula failed to build or rehabilitate a road in the area. Sefateng was in partial compliance with its community water and schools support projects. The report also found that the department of minerals, resources and energy failed to hold mining companies accountable. “The state is obliged to take measures towards the realisation of rights enshrined in chapter 2 of the constitution [such as education, food, water, healthcare and social security]. The state’s failure to ensure compliance not only leads to the violation of these rights but creates an untenable situation in which companies have free rein to do as they please,” said Louis Snyman from the centre for applied legal studies. “A department of minerals, resources and energy official sent email responses to a member of the research team stating that they had not yet received a compliance report by Sefateng for the year 2020 but that, even worse, Sefateng have not been submitting annual compliance reports generally. “The mine has continued to operate despite being a persistent offender in failing to report [according to the department] as expressly required by the minerals and petroleum resources development act.” The report also found that not a single interviewee from the affected communities recounted a positive experience of the minerals and energy department. “The common thread among the interviewees was very minimal presence and contact of the department of minerals, resources and energy in their communities. The department was not perceived as an institution that looks after the rights of communities, nor one to hold mining companies accountable to their SLP obligations,” read the report. Another damning finding against the energy department was that it only has 96 Environmental Mineral Resources Inspectors tasked with monitoring mines’ compliance with their environmental obligations. “This is grossly insufficient given the 1,757 operational mines across the country,” read the report. Amnesty International researcher Marike Keller said the researchers wrote to the mining companies and minerals and energy department to present them with findings of the report and to seek responses on a number of issues raised in the report. Keller said only Anglo American and Sefateng responded but there were no responses received from Impala Platinum Mine nor the department at the time the report was finalised for print.  Would you like to comment on this article or view other readers' comments? Register (it’s quick and free) or sign in now. Please read our Comment Policy before commenting.

results.plain_text: There can be few people of any age in Britain today unfamiliar with “Blowin’ in the Wind”, a song that will be 60 years old next year. Many will know the Bob Dylan original; others versions by Peter, Paul and Mary, Stevie Wonder, Elvis Presley, Lena Horne, Marlene Dietrich or Ziggy Marley. Some may know it from church, others from Forrest Gump or a Co-Op ad. Fans of Douglas Adams will remember the song’s opening line (“How many roads must a man walk down?”) as the “Ultimate Question” in The Hitch-Hiker’s Guide to the Galaxy. Much the same question was posed by Pope John Paul II after Dylan had sung the song at the 23rd Italian National Eucharistic Congress. Crowds sang it at protests against the Iraq War in 2003 as they had at anti-Vietnam war protests in the 60s, and during the bitter battle for civil rights in the US. Your guide to what to watch next - no spoilers, we promise It is true that much popular music has endured – at any moment of the day a DJ somewhere is playing 60s classics. But Dylan – who will turn 80 on Monday – occupies a special place not just in music but in 20th-century history. His legacy is most apparent in the legions of “composers” who endeavour to create meaningfully emotive rock songs to be sung with a drummer nearby, emphasising the downbeat. Asked recently to complete the sentence “without Bob Dylan there would be no…”, I added “idiosyncratic untrained vocalists singing about existential angst”. Facile but true. David Gray, Ed Sheeran and Laura Marling are among countless singers indebted to Dylan. For it was Dylan who democratised popular song, his desert boots trampling over long- established and formulaic Tin Pan Alley traditions, in the process changing the metabolism of popular music. While he famously described himself as just “a song and dance man”, he refused early on to be a music-business puppet, storming off the set of The Ed Sullivan Show rather than compromise on his choice of song, and pushing on relentlessly as fans booed and accused him of betrayal when he swapped his acoustic guitar and work-shirt for a Fender Stratocaster and leather jacket. “Almost single-handedly, Dylan took poetry off the dusty shelves and put it on the jukebox,” wrote Robert Shelton, the New York Times critic whose review of “a bright new face in folk music” playing at Gerde’s Folk City in September 1961 launched his career. Shelton was Dylan’s first biographer, beginning work on the study that would become No Direction Home in early 1966. Uniquely, he was granted access to Dylan’s parents, whom no other journalist interviewed; to friends from his Hibbing childhood and his student days in Minneapolis; to musicians and poets with whom he had scuffled for dimes in Greenwich Village. From the outset, Shelton recognised Dylan’s extraordinary songwriting talent (“the singing poet laureate of young America” was how he described him in 1963) and he wanted his book to treat him as one of the 20th century’s great shape-shifters, to be spoken of in the same breath as Shaw, Picasso and Welles. “There is scarcely a popular music form that Dylan didn’t infuse with new possibilities,” he wrote, describing him as “a master politician as well as influencer”. Today’s “influencers” serve a different (and far more ignoble) function, but in the pre-wired world in which he came to fame, Dylan’s influence was as vast as it was unpremeditated. “Don’t follow leaders”, he sang in 1965’s “Subterranean Homesick Blues” – accompanied by the first music video, 16 years before the launch of MTV – but the world’s youth followed him nonetheless. Presenting him with a Kennedy Centre award in 1997, Bill Clinton thanked Dylan for “a lifetime of stirring the conscience of the nation” – a view with which House Speaker Newt Gingrich, Clinton’s nemesis, agreed. Clinton, who, as a presidential candidate faced tricky questions about his draft deferment, said that Dylan provided those who protested against the Vietnam war “with a moral compass as accessible as the nearest radio”. Dylan never specifically spoke out against the Vietnam war, but songs such as “Masters of War” and “The Times They Are a-Changin’” took clear aim at the military-industrial complex and at the senators and congressmen, mothers and fathers, quick to criticise “what they can’t understand”. The shockwave was in part demographic. With more teenagers on the streets, James Dean represented the iconic potential of a rebellious cohort. Dylan taught it to protest. Unlike Joan Baez, who introduced songs by her then-unknown vagabond lover to a ready-made audience, Dylan never took to the barricades. But he gave teenagers – the real baby boomers, like Clinton, born in the years immediately following the Second World War – what Baez called their musical “arsenal”. His period as “a topical songwriter” was brief but productive: the songs he wrote in five-cent notebooks in Village coffee houses speak to us with the same urgency today as they did to Clinton – think of “With God On Our Side” and “A Hard Rain’s ­a-Gonna Fall”, Dylan’s nightmare war vision written as the world stared into the abyss during the Cuban Missile Crisis. Through songs such as “The Ballad of Medgar Evers” and “The ­Lonesome Death of Hattie Carroll”, the grim waystations of America’s bloody civil rights struggle entered global consciousness. Like the troubadours and ballad-mongers of old, Dylan’s early songs provided us with history lessons. They gave voice to our grievances and our grieving. As Barack Obama declared in 2012 as he hung the Presidential Medal of Freedom around his neck, Dylan redefined “not just what music sounded like, but the message it carried and how it made people feel”. When the 2008 Pulitzer Prize Board honoured Dylan, it was with a special citation acknowledging his “profound impact on popular music and American culture, marked by lyrical compositions of extraordinary poetic power”. More than anyone else, Dylan developed the Esperanto of the rock community – as David Bowie wrote in “Song to Bobby” on Hunky Dory, “While troubles are rising, we’d rather be scared together than alone”. It was “A Hard Rain” that inspired a young Canadian poet named Leonard Cohen to try his hand at songwriting. In the 70s, Bruce Springsteen and Patti Smith were each marketed as “the new Dylan”. Joni Mitchell, Lou Reed, Don McLean, Kurt Cobain, Tracy Chapman – no rock style or rock star has been untouched by Dylan, not even The Beatles. In the early 60s, they, too, sported Huck Finn caps, and “Working-Class Hero”, John Lennon’s first post-Beatles song, is a direct lift. Dylan’s work is part of the woof and warp of popular culture; lines from his great corpus of songs are used daily by headline writers. How smart he was, observing that “money doesn’t talk, it swears”, or that someone could be “helpless like a rich man’s child”. A group of Swedish research scientists routinely use Dylan lyrics in their articles, among them “Tangled Up in Blue: Molecular Cardiology in the Post-molecular Era”. Dylan’s reputation rests on a mighty handful of albums made before he turned 25, including Freewheelin’, Bringing it All Back Home and Blonde on Blonde, the electric albums fusing extraordinary lyrics with what he called “that thin, wild mercury sound”. Each subsequent decade included one brilliant Dylan album: Blood on the Tracks (1975), Oh Mercy (1989) and Time Out of Mind (1997). Released amid the first Covid lockdown, Rough and Rowdy Ways, his first all-original outing since 2012, surprised a world which had assumed that Dylan’s creative muse had deserted him. It won widespread acclaim, analysis of his song “Murder Most Foul”, about the Kennedy assassination, occupying endless column inches. The New Yorker described the album as “a reckoning with the vastness and immediacy of American culture”. Along the way, Dylan has painted, sculpted, presented a much-praised radio series, and launched a range of bespoke whiskies. He has also raised eyebrows by licensing some of his most famous songs for use in TV ads in which he has sometimes appeared. Still, it could be said that in the endless commodification of his legacy, he has behaved like many others of his generation – selling his song catalogue for upwards of $300m (£212m) last December was simply the rock star’s equivalent of cashing in the corporate pension. But then he has often been contradictory – or as he puts it on his latest album, “I Contain Multitudes”. The line is from Walt Whitman, who hung out in Greenwich Village 100 years before Dylan, throwing off the shackles of poetic form and writing free verse in a voice uniquely American. Many say that without Whitman, you cannot fully understand America. Without Dylan, you cannot fully understand the 20th century. Bob Dylan: No Direction Home by Robert Shelton, a new illustrated edition with a foreword and afterword by Elizabeth Thomson, is published by Palazzo Editions (£30). Thomson is the author of Joan Baez: The Last Leaf (Palazzo Editions, £14.99)

Uniquely, he was granted access to Dylan’s parents, whom no other journalist interviewed; to friends from his Hibbing childhood and his student days in Minneapolis; to musicians and poets with whom he had scuffled for dimes in Greenwich Village.

results.actual_text: ădăcinile acestor cratoni. Aceştia au asamblat atunci o machetă din roci confecţionate din
results.plain_text: Cercetătorii americani au descoperit miliarde de tone de diamante, dar nu pot ajunge la ele întrucât se află sub suprafața Terrei la o adâncime la care nu se pot face săpături, scrie AFP citând un studiu realizat de oamenii de știință de la Massachusetts Institute of Technology, scrie libertatea.ro. Studiile arată că această comoară estimată la un milion de miliarde de tone de diamante se găsește la o distanță cuprinsă între 145 şi 240 de kilometri de suprafața Terrei, cu mult peste distanțele atinse de echipamentele de foraj moderne. "Nu putem ajunge la ele, dar sunt mai multe diamante decât ne imaginăm", a spus Ulrich Faul, cercetător în cadrul departamentului de ştiinţe planetare, atmosferice şi ale Pământului în cadrul MIT. Descoperirea oamenilor de știință arată că diamantul nu este un mineral exotic ci, la scară planetară, este un mineral întâlnit frecvent. Cercetătorii de la universitatea americană au ajuns la concluzia existenței diamantelor folosind tehnologia seismică pentru a analiza felul în care undele sonore traversează scoarța terestră. Proiectul a demarat după ce cercetătorii au fost uimiţi de faptul că undele sonore accelerau într-o manieră semnificativă atunci când traversau rădăcinile acestor cratoni. Aceştia au asamblat atunci o machetă din roci confecţionate din diferite combinaţii de minerale pentru a calcula viteza cu care vor fi traversate de undele sonore. "Diamantul este special din mai multe puncte de vedere. Una dintre proprietăţile sale este că sunetul îl traversează cu o viteză de două ori mai mare decât în cazul mineralelor care predomină în rocile din mantaua superioară, şi anume olivina", a mai explicat cercetătorul. Oamenii de ştiinţă cred acum că rocile subterane conţin de cel puţin 1.000 de ori mai multe diamante decât s-a crezut iniţial. Cu toate acestea, pietrele preţioase descoperite mai au mult până să ajungă pe rafturile bijuteriilor. Formate din carbon, diamantele iau naştere la o presiune foarte mare şi la o temperatură extremă în adâncul pământului şi nu ies la suprafaţă decât prin intermediul erupţiilor vulcanice, fenomen care nu are loc foarte des.

results.actual_text: At the Special Olympics World Games, the world comes together to give intellectually challenged athletes the
results.plain_text: At the Special Olympics World Games, the world comes together to give intellectually challenged athletes the opportunity to push limits and break barriers through sport. Limits for these athletes come in the form of intellectual disabilities (ID) and the stigma and discrimination which too often accompany them – even when it comes to healthcare. Despite being more vulnerable to health risks, the intellectually disabled often receive less than high-quality health assistance. Miscommunication between patients and doctors, or simply inadequate health assessment, are common faults of the system. In addition, coaching staff at the Olympics told Euronews that ID patients are often too intimidated to speak up, leading to health conditions being under-reported. This was the impetus behind an initiative by the Special Olympics, founded in 1997, which offers athletes free health screenings and information during competitions. Healthy Athletes' success stories are striking and often heartwarming – such as that of a 31-year-old Senegalese footballer who got to hear for the very first time thanks to treatment at the competition, or that of a little boy with Down’s syndrome who was diagnosed with a limb-threatening foot condition. The route to change: training healthcare providers “One of the things we like to say here is that we have bi-directional learning,” said Healthy Athletes' director, Meghan McHugh. Along with providing health screenings, the organisation educates Special athletes on how to be healthy, using an interactive and fun approach, but they also educate healthcare providers. The failings of the healthcare system are confirmed by data collected by the organisation. McHugh said: “Nine per cent of the athletes are in pain and 44 per cent have untreated tooth decay.” “We show healthcare providers how to effectively communicate with ID folks – like not finishing their sentences, using pictures, giving them more time at the doctor’s office," explains McHugh. “We need athletes and ID patients to feel included, to feel comfortable at the doctor’s office, to think it's somewhere where they can be heard.” “Being healthy should not be scary. It’s critical to provide an environment that’s welcoming,” she adds. The wrong prescription for glasses or shoes which are too tight are common examples of issues that could be easily solved by small adjustments in the examination process. “We strive for a sustainable change, we don’t want a temporary one just for this week, and that’s why we want to change the curriculum,” said McHugh. Mindfulness at the Special Olympics The athletes are also being instructed in the field of stress management: “Like the rest of society, they have high levels of stress. And also, sometimes people are very mean to them,” adds McHugh. According to Healthy Athletes’ screenings, only 12 per cent of the athletes have any knowledge of stress management techniques – a gap the initiative is addressing. “Healthy Athletes is teaching the Special Olympic athletes stress management techniques such as colouring, yoga, stretching, breathing techniques and even mindfulness,” said McHugh. “We look forward to the checks and teachings, it improves not only their performance at the games but also their daily lives. These in-depth specialised check-ups don’t happen back home”. The message to the medical community is clear. “Choose to work with people with intellectual disabilities. They’re wonderful people, and they deserve access to high-quality healthcare,” concludes McHugh.

Along with providing health screenings, the organisation educates Special athletes on how to be healthy, using an interactive and fun approach, but they also educate healthcare providers.

These in-depth specialised check-ups don’t happen back home”.

results.actual_text: Sweden’s chief epidemiologist Anders Tegnell has warned against the strategy of attaining
results.plain_text: Sweden’s chief epidemiologist Anders Tegnell has warned against the strategy of attaining herd immunity in apparent change of heart. In March, Dr Tegnell appeared to ask whether a higher death rate among older people might be acceptable if it led to faster herd immunity. However in comments to German newspaper Die Zeit, Dr Tegnell stated “striving for herd immunity is neither ethical nor otherwise justifiable”. Sweden’s daily coronavirus infections have climbed to a new high while several regions advised residents to avoid public transport and venues to slow the transmission. The Swedish Public Health Agency said that 3254 cases were confirmed in the past 24 hours, a new record since the onset of the pandemic. It was the third record number in a matter of days and came as the country of 10.3 million has registered roughly 121,100 infections and 5934 coronavirus-related deaths in total. Sweden has until recently had “a fairly slow increase (of infections) compared to most other countries,” Dr Tegnell said at a press conference. “We’re beginning to approach the ceiling for what the healthcare system can handle. “Together, as during the spring, we can push down this curve and avoid the strain on healthcare.” Dr Tegnell said the Swedish system of “voluntary measures” had worked well and many people heeded the recommendations. The number of COVID-19 patients in intensive care units (ICU) was still relatively low – about 15 per cent of the current ICU capacity, said Thomas Linden with the National Board of Health and Welfare. Sweden has shunned lockdowns throughout the pandemic. However, several regions – including the most populous areas comprising the main cities of Stockholm and Gothenburg in western Sweden – advised residents to refrain from visits to shopping centres, museums, libraries, swimming pools and fitness centres until November 18. Other advice was for people to avoid meeting with others outside their own household and to work from home where possible, Dr Tegnell said. During the pandemic, Sweden has kept schools and kindergartens open but limited public gatherings to 50 participants, visits in care homes were until recently banned and self service in restaurants and bars suspended. -with agencies
